watch_reasons: Accepted. Salt Lake is retained as the domestic salt-lake potassium and lithium anchor. Its moat comes from Qarhan salt-lake resource access; high margins; brine and mining-right constraints; and a role not covered by hard-rock lithium peers. | Retained. Salt Lake is not a global potash monopoly. It has a hard-to-replicate domestic strategic-resource position in Qarhan salt-lake potash and salt-lake lithium; very high 2025 margins; large mining-right area after resource integration; and capacity constrained by scarce brine resources and permits.
calibrated_standard_implications: Domestic salt-lake lithium and potassium platforms can qualify without global monopoly when resource access is scarce; margins are structurally supported; and the advantage is not mainly lithium-price beta. | A domestic monopoly or near-monopoly can qualify when the mineral is import-sensitive; resource access is scarce; margins are structurally high; and the advantage is not mainly commodity-price beta.

watch_reasons: Revised to watch after reviewer challenge. In a lithium-only comparison Zangge is weaker than Salt Lake; however at listed-company level it combines potash cash flow; salt-lake lithium optionality; a material Julong Copper stake whose 2025 investment income dominated profit; and Zijin's new controlling-shareholder role. This cross-resource package is hard to replicate and deserves observation while risks from copper price beta; equity-method earnings; and less direct operating control than Zijin must be tracked.
calibrated_standard_implications: A company rejected inside a narrow peer group must be rechecked for cross-industry scarce-resource assets or controlling-shareholder capability.
